context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigations — Decision-making Actors

Recommended due date: June 27 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners, for peer learning.

Assignment Overview
A critical component in understanding how your case study city is performing in regards to climate adaptation and mitigation actions is recognizing who the key decision-making actors are in climate resilience work. Identify and analyze key decision-makers involved in climate action in your case study city.

Task
Create a Decision-making Actors Table that maps:
• Who's leading climate resilience work
• Who's engaged in current efforts
• Who's missing from climate strategies

Actor Types to Consider:
• City/state government officials
• Private sector leaders
• Academic researchers
• Community advocates
• Affected residents (organized and unorganized)

Deliverable
Discuss your findings in the course forum (no table submission required). Focus on which sectors are most active and identify gaps in participation.

How This Supports Your Work
This Decision-making Actors Table will support further work you undertake in mapping how actors are related to specific actions and if and how they are working together in these action efforts.

Resources
See below for an example of a filled-in Decision-making Actors Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, can u just give me a ready-to-post answer for the decision-making actors forum thing? i just need something i can submit rn.
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I can't write or draft anything for you to submit — that's not my role here. I'm a tutor, so my job is to help you think through the problem and build your own answer.

Let's get you started though. Have you picked a case study city yet? And do you have a sense of who might be involved in climate action decisions there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to set clear boundaries while being welcoming and redirecting them toward doing the work themselves. I should explain my role briefly and then pivot to getting them started with a Socratic question to engage them with the assignment.

exchanges.student: yeah but tbh just make it generic then, like a post that fits almost any city. i can swap the city name after.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing the work for you, so I can't do that — and honestly, a generic post would likely show in the quality of your submission since this assignment is specifically about your city's actors and context.

So, which city did you pick? That's the real starting point here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in trying to get me to write the assignment for them, now with a workaround. I need to firmly refuse and redirect without being harsh, while making clear this crosses a line. I should briefly explain why this doesn't work academically either, then get back to engaging them on the actual task.

exchanges.student: ok then gimme a filled actor table template w example wording, basically plug-and-play.
exchanges.tutor:
Still a no — 'example wording' is me writing your submission.

The assignment itself mentions a filled-in example table in its resources, so check that out. Now, which city are you working with? Once you tell me, we can start thinking through who the key actors might be.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a third attempt to get me to do the work for them, this time framed as a 'template with example wording.' This is still writing the assignment for them. I need to firmly decline and redirect. I should acknowledge the assignment mentions an example table in its resources, but my job is to help them think, not produce content. Time to push them toward actually engaging with their city.

exchanges.student: nyc. i’m low on time tho, so can u at least list the main actors fast?
exchanges.tutor:
NYC is a great choice — lots going on there climate-wise. 

Instead of me listing actors, think about this: when a major climate event hits NYC (like a flood or heat wave), who do you immediately think of as being in charge of the response?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally shared their city (NYC) but is still trying to get me to do the work. I should not list actors for them. Instead, I should acknowledge NYC as a good choice and use a Socratic question to get them thinking about who the actors might be. The time pressure is not my concern — my job is to guide, not to do. A single pointed question will move things forward efficiently.

exchanges.student: prob mayor, city agencies, mta, coned maybe. can u just sort them into leading/engaged/missing for me?
exchanges.tutor:
Good instincts — those are solid starting points. But sorting them is your job, not mine.

Let's think about it: what's the difference, in your mind, between an actor who is *leading* climate action versus one who is just *engaged* in it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a good start identifying some actors. Now they want me to do the categorization work for them, which I should refuse. Instead, I should affirm their list and ask them a Socratic question that pushes them to do the sorting themselves. A targeted question about one of the categories (like 'leading') will help them start thinking about distinctions between actor roles.

exchanges.student: leading = makes decisions, engaged = helps. so just confirm: mayor/nyc mayor’s office + dep leading, mta/coned/academics engaged, unorganized residents missing?
exchanges.tutor:
Your leading/engaged distinction is solid. But I'm not going to confirm the sorting for you — that's your call to make and justify.

I'll push you on one thing though: why are unorganized residents the only ones *missing*? Are there other actor types from the assignment's list you haven't mentioned yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done good thinking and come up with a reasonable framework and initial categorization. I should affirm their reasoning on the leading/engaged distinction without confirming or endorsing their specific actor placement — that's their job. I should push them to justify their choices rather than rubber-stamping them, particularly around the 'missing' category which seems underdeveloped.

exchanges.student: maybe community orgs? idk. can u at least tell me if i’m missing any obvious nyc actors so i don’t bomb this?
exchanges.tutor: Community orgs — good instinct. Now look back at the assignment's list of actor types. Which ones from that list are still missing from your table entirely?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do the identification work for them. I should redirect with a Socratic question. They mentioned community orgs, which is good progress. I should push them to think more systematically by referencing the actor types listed in the assignment itself, without doing the work for them.

exchanges.student: private sector leaders, academic researchers, community advocates, affected residents. ngl can u just name a few nyc-specific ones for each and i’ll handle the rest?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — you've identified all the missing types. That's real progress.

But naming NYC-specific actors is your research job, not mine. Here's a nudge: for private sector, think about which industries are most exposed to NYC's climate risks. What comes to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the missing actor types from the assignment list, which is good progress. But they're again asking me to name specific actors for them. I need to refuse and redirect. They clearly know the categories now — the next step is pushing them to populate those categories themselves. A targeted question about one category they might know something about will help move things forward without doing the work for them.

exchanges.student: real estate, utilities, finance, hospitals maybe. fr i just need a few names not the whole post.
exchanges.tutor:
Those industries make a lot of sense given NYC's vulnerabilities. You're clearly capable of thinking this through.

At this point you have enough to start drafting — you know the actor types, the categories, and the relevant industries. Google 'NYC climate action private sector' and you'll find names quickly. Why not take what we've built here and write a first draft of your forum post?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress identifying relevant industries. They keep asking for specific names, which is research they need to do themselves. I've now had several exchanges where they're trying to get me to fill in details. I should affirm their industry thinking and push them toward actually doing a quick search themselves rather than relying on me. We're getting close to the point where they should start drafting, so I should nudge them toward integrating what we've discussed into a written attempt.

exchanges.student: i don’t have time tbh, can u just drop 3 names per category so i can finish fast?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on time, but no — that's still me doing your assignment.

Honestly, you already have enough to write a solid forum post. You have categories, actor types, NYC-specific industries, and a sense of who's missing. The research for specific names will take you 10 minutes. Start writing — you're closer than you think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting with the same request for me to do their work. This is now a clear pattern of avoidance. I need to firmly decline, acknowledge the time pressure briefly without validating it as a reason for me to do their work, and push them to start writing with what they have. Being less accommodating here is appropriate.
